title: Giant of the Skies Unveiled
story: A massive metal bird spread its wings in 1968. The Lockheed C-5A Galaxy, the world's largest plane at the time, was proudly shown off by the U.S. Air Force on March 2. This flying giant could swallow cargo like no other, with a belly stretching nearly the length of a basketball court. It could fly to the moon

story: Beneath Europa's icy surface lies a secret. In 1998, on March 2, the Galileo spacecraft made an exciting discovery about Jupiter's moon. title: Naomi James: Sailing Solo Around the World
story: Naomi James was born in New Zealand in 1949, destined for adventure on the high seas. This brave sailor made history as the first woman to sail solo around the world via the treacherous Cape Horn. James' groundbreaking voyage inspired countless others to chase their dreams, no matter how big.

First woman to fly a plane around the world

story: Boom! The sound of cannons echoed across Boston in 1776. On March 2, American troops fired their first shots at the British soldiers occupying the city. This bold move marked a turning point in the fight for independence. The colonists had secretly moved heavy guns into position, catching the enemy by surprise.
activity_question: What did American troops begin doing to the British in Boston?
follow_up_question: Why do you think the Americans waited until nighttime to move their cannons?

title: Speedy Signals: The Semaphore Revolution
story: Imagine sending messages without phones or computers! In 1791, a clever machine called the semaphore was unveiled in Paris on March 2. This invention used moving arms to spell out messages across long distances. Operators would watch through telescopes and relay signals from tower to tower, creating a fast communication network across France.
activity_question: What type of machine was unveiled in Paris?
follow_up_question: How did operators read the semaphore signals from far away?

title: Baseball's Big Rule Change
story: Batter up! In 1874, baseball got serious about fair play. The National Association of Professional Baseball Players made some game-changing rules on March 2. They officially added the batter's box to the field. But that wasn't all. They also cracked down on gambling. Players caught betting on their own team would be kicked out for good. Betting on other teams? Say goodbye to your paycheck!
activity_question: What would happen to players caught betting on their own team?
activity_choices: They would sit out one game
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important for sports to have rules against gambling?

title: Safety First on the Rails
story: Choo-choo! In 1893, the United States took a big step to make train travel safer. On March 2, the government passed its first law about railroads. This new rule said trains had to have special safety features. It was like giving trains seatbelts and airbags to protect passengers and workers.
activity_question: What did the new law require trains to have?
follow_up_question: Why do you think safety on trains is important?

title: Hockey Drama: The Tie That Wasn't
story: Skates clashed and sticks flew as the Montreal Wanderers faced off against Ottawa HC in 1904. The Stanley Cup match ended in a thrilling 5-5 tie on March 2. But the excitement turned to controversy when Montreal refused to play the second game in Ottawa. Their stubbornness led to disqualification, leaving fans buzzing about the unexpected twist in hockey history.
activity_question: What was the final score of the Stanley Cup match?
follow_up_question: Why do you think Montreal refused to play the second game in Ottawa?
